sentences.text: Yes.
I entered Warsaw Ghetto [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the end of August or the beginning of September 1942.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was not difficult.
Warsaw, at that time, in the Warsaw ghetto, which originally housed some 450,000 Jews, when I entered it, there were no more 60,000, 70,000.
The rest, already deported to Treblinka and, of course, murdered over there.
Now, as a matter of fact, there were four little ghettos on the territory of that big ghetto surrounded by a wall.
And each one [INAUDIBLE] specialty.
Cobblers or who-- who-- whatsoever, you know, because they had to work.
Between those four little ghettos-- areas [INAUDIBLE] to which already Aryans moved-- Aryans meaning Poles, gentiles.
Because the deported Jews left empty houses.
There was shortage of houses.
So the Germans allowed the Poles to move in.
Gestapo had one of its officers in that Aryan part of the ghetto.
Tramways were passing between them.
Only you were not allowed to get off of the tramway on the territory of the ghetto.
Yeah.
So to enter the ghetto was not so difficult.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, I saw terrible things, starvation and humiliation, naked bodies, only the middle covered by some newspaper.
When I asked my guide what does it mean, he says, the Germans want us to pay for burial.
There is no money.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, every-- every shred of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] vestment is needed.
So, you know, naked body-- only covered the middle.
I saw horrible, horrible things.
Women feeding babies, but they had no breast, you know.
Horrible.
I saw horror-- I saw too much.
Hmm?
The first time, leader of a sociologist underground, his name was Leon Finer.
Finer.
Now, he accompanied me to give me courage that I wouldn't afraid you know?
He was telling me, don't be afraid.
It is not so dangerous.
I will be your guide.
Now, again, I couldn't stand it very much.
I never saw in my life this kind, you know, of people, this kind of environment.
It was not my world-- world.
You know, they were not humans.
So we left the ghetto.
But when we came on the street, I told him, I want to come back.
And I did come back two or three days later.
But he didn't come.
He gave me somebody else as a guide.
Now, when I entered for the first time, we entered through the tunnel under a basement of an apartment house who-- which formed a part of a wall.
The back to the street, the front was to the ghetto.
And there was a young boy.
I didn't pay attention.
And he was following us, because we were walking the streets.
And I didn't pay any attention.
Then some seven, eight months ago, I went on some lecture tour to Australia, to Melbourne.
The press wrote about me.
And his name is David Landau.
He came.
He says, I saw you entering the ghetto.
[INAUDIBLE] He survived.
And now, he's one of the richer men in Australia.
I love it.
[LAUGH] He gave me chess.
[INAUDIBLE] The end of the 19th century-- ivory.
He paid, in my presence, $5,000 Canadian.
[LAUGH] For him, it is nothing, this little Jewish boy in the ghetto.
Ah.
Well, so, then I left Warsaw in the middle, probably-- no, the beginning of November 1942, this time going to London.
I arrived in London the second part of November with two kinds of messages.
First, messages from the Polish underground-- quasi-underground parliament.
The representatives of the four main political prewar parties-- Peasant Party, Nationalist Party, Socialist, and Christian Democratic Party, they formed a semi-- unofficial, underground parliament.
Also, messages from the delegate of the government, who at that time was Cyryl Ratajski, and some individuals in the underground.
Then in the meantime, the Jews got in touch with me.
They learned about my mission.
And they asked the delegate to ask me, would I carry their messages on the same basis as the messages of other Polish political parties.
Of course, he agreed.
I agreed.
I met them twice.
One was-- identified himself as a socialist leader from Bund.
His identity is not doubted, because I described him.
I remember his feature, you know.
And many people knew him.
He had a beautiful face, mustachios, the-- a little to the-- up, like a Swedish 17th-century aristocracy, beautiful face, walking straight, rosy cheeks.
He looked classical Polish nobleman, you know, estate owner.
And he was in hiding, you know, under this-- he-- they told me he supposedly owned some little factories.
So he was well-to-do.
What was pathetic-- when he said he will go with me.
So he told me, for I will give you the sign when.
But in the meantime, don't shave.
Prepare all this, so to not to be recognizable, et cetera.
And then we met, go to the ghetto and enter the ghetto.
This was pathetic, that Polish nobleman, entering the ghetto, you know, humble, humiliated Jew-- [INAUDIBLE] Jewish.
You suddenly think, in  the whole 1,000-years history of Jewish persecution, you know.
He played, you know.
Then he became a Jew of 2,000 years of the diaspora.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was Leon Finer.
He died.
After the war, as a matter of fact, he died.
He survived.
Yes.
I had several messages from the Jews.
And they, of course, insisted.
They realized that Polish government is weak.
So they begged me, begged me, to approach and-- as many leaders, but allied leaders, English or American or whomever, French, whomever.
And I was good for them.
Because they said, you speak languages.
You are smart.
You are clever.
Tell them.
Tell them.
And they giving me those messages, which I tried to pass, various messages.
Let the Allies make a public declaration that stopping persecution of the Jews became a part of the Allied war strategy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yes.
When I would say it to British officials, impossible.
They said that such a declaration is impossible.
First, Goebbels is saying all the time that this war was provoked by the Jews, is managed by the Jews, in behalf of the Jews.
If we make such a declaration, Karski, who knows?
Perhaps some of our own people might think perhaps Goebbels is right.
Why only the Jews?
Then he said, but what about what the French will say, or Belgians?
Why they are not mentioned?
Why only the Jews?
I remember one of them looking at me.
Mr.
Karski, can you assure us that your own people will not resent it?
They also suffer.
Even more important, what the Russians will say?
They have tremendous losses.
We cannot say that stopping persecution of the Jews became our war strategy.
Impossible.
[INAUDIBLE] other messages, also.
Let them-- let them announce that certain German historically valuable objects will be bombed, destroyed, as retaliation for what their government is doing to the Jews.
That-- that bombing will continue as long as the Nazi government will pursue its policy of destruction of the Jews, the argument is.
But this is a silly argument.
We cannot do it.
But if you do it, we will justify German bombing of our civilian objects.
Germans are doing this.
And we protest all over the world, that they are not-- that there are rules of war.
They are not supposed to do it.
Now, if we publicly stated that we will do it, it means we justify interaction.
We cannot do it.
Whatever I would touch, this object, the argument would be-- which seemed to me rational-- but every argument of such a nature is precluding any help.
Such was the situation.
The problem was, you know, and this-- since this is televised, I want the, you know, viewers to realize the Jews had no chance during the war.
They had no chance.
They were doomed.

Because at that time, on the Allied side, such tremendous issues were, you know, saving humanity, defeating Hitler, unconditional surrender, destruction, German war potential, cooperation with the Russians, with Stalin, of course, et cetera.
At the same time, on the Jewish side, keep in mind the Jews had no their own country.
They had no government.
They had no army.
They were-- they had no representative in the inter-Allied war council.
They had no voice.
You understand it.
So they relied on some others.
In my case, little agent from Poland, Karski, that he will say.
But those others, most probably many of them were sympathetic towards the Jews.
But I have no doubt some of them were unsympathetic towards the Jews.
The Jews were totally helpless.
Next, what happened to the Jews also is important to those who will ever see our interview.
What happened to the Jew, when I told them what I saw, was unprecedented in the human history.
It never happened before.
And for educated men, elite-- and I met, of course, many were from elite.
In England, there is mostly aristocracy.
Lord Cranborn, Lord Selborn, Eden, they had best education.
They never saw these kind of things.
It was even difficult for them, probably, really to believe.
I had a feeling at that time that in some cases the men-- I had this feeling.
He thinks that I am spreading exaggerated, anti-German propaganda.
I remember, once I made a report to Lord Selborn.
He was a supervisor of all underground movements in Europe.
He decided who should get help, who should be ignored, a powerful man.
He left a memo once stating his activities.
I report to him what I saw.
Then he listened.
And then he-- he says, Mr.
Karski, you know, during the First World War, there were rumors all over Europe that the German soldiers in Belgium liked to fetch the babies by their feet and crush their skulls against the wall.
Now, of course, we knew those rumors were not-- untrue.
But we didn't do anything to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] correct them.
They were good for the morale of our people.
And then-- Mr.
Karski, speak what you saw.
Inform as many people as possible.
We'll stand behind you.
You are doing a great work.
Of course, I'm not particularly proud of this incident.
Perhaps if it happened now, at my age, I would ask Your Lordship, why did you tell me this little story?
At the time, I didn't.
I was a nobody.
My job was not to antagonize, to provoke.
You understand.
I-- I was a little man.
Then I was also under orders from my government.
And the government, then Sikorski and his deputy, Mikolajczyk, were telling me before every meeting, be careful.
He's a powerful man.
Don't provoke.
Don't antagonize.
We need his help, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] particularly the Jews.
Very often, I am embarrassed.
Because they exaggerate my role.
They make out of me, you know, an important man during the war.
I was a nobody.
I was a tape recorder.
I was a mailing box.
In what way-- what was my leverage, talking to Eden?
Or, I approach four members of the British war cabinet.
This was the center of power, Lord Cranborn, Conservative Party, Arthur Greenwood, Labour Party.
Hugh Dalton, Labour Party.
In the United States, Roosevelt, Cordell Hull, Francis Biddle, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Henry Stimson, Secretary of War, you know, with this kind of people, I had no leverage.
You understand it.
I was waiting for questions.
And if a question was asked, I would answer if I knew the answer.
But I couldn't ask questions.
People ask me, so you talked to Roosevelt.
What did Roosevelt do?
What was his reaction?
Yes, he told me-- he-- he-- he [INAUDIBLE] he-- at that time, he was informed that I was going back to Poland by the Polish ambassador, who by diplomatic protocol, introduced me to the President.
So he knew [INAUDIBLE].
When he gave me his hand, this is the end.
So I was sort of inspired.
And I wanted to pin him down a little.
So I told him, Mr.
President, I am going to Poland.
Every leader will know that I spoke to President Roosevelt.
Sir, everybody will ask me, what did the President tell you?
Mr.
President, what do I answer?
And I remember.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 'You will tell your leaders that we shall win this war.' He-- he continuously smoked a long cigarette, a long cigarette holder-- 'that we will win this war.
The guilty ones will be punished for their crimes.
Justice, freedom, shall prevail.
You will tell your nation that they have a friend in this house.
This is what you will tell them.' And gave me his hand.
I couldn't tell him, Mr.
President, that's all?
You understand?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I just bowed.
The ambassador laughed at me, cracking joke, for years.
He said, that I was afraid that you will break-- break the desk, because you bowed so low.
I approached the door.
Also, he told me, I didn't realize-- ambassador told me, Johnny, do you know, you were walking to the door backward.
I didn't dare to turn my back to the President.
For me, he was lord of humanity.
He did project greatness, Roosevelt.
He had tremendous personality.
No, never.
No.
In Washington, DC.
And then, also, you mentioned the war.
The war ended May the 5.

[INAUDIBLE] every day, newspapers are reporting, American statesmen, ambassadors, university professors, rectors, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] political leaders, go to Europe to see what happened in Germany, you know, to see concentration camps.
News spread.
And then return, practically everybody-- I saw terrible, terrible, terrible things.
We didn't know what was happening.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I got disgusted.
They knew.
This version that they didn't know is a myth.
They knew.
I-- as much as this is untrue, that I was the first one, or, you know-- they were well-informed.
They knew.
Only the Jewish problem was insignificant.
On this basis, sometime, at Georgetown University, when in the last 10 years my name became known-- everybody noticed it.
So Jewish boys come to my class, you know?
[LAUGH] I realize very well why, you know?
They expect A, you know?
Karski said, good going.
[LAUGH] They-- Karski-- but I mention them, because sometime girls or boy would come to my office with a question.
Professor Karski, I read your book, you know.
And people talk about it.
Professor Karski, is another Holocaust possible?
To everybody, I say, don't be crazy.
Out of the question.
Do you believe them?
Oh, I denied it.
This is ridiculous even as a question.
Not because humanity changed.
Humanity did not change.
We know what happened in Cambodia, Somalia, Ethiopia, Bosnia and Herzegovina.
Everything changed.
As far as the Jews are concerned, there is Israel.
See, this is the change.
Holocaust is impossible.
Any country who would start Holocaust, well, I don't want to continue what would happen.
[LAUGH] No Holocaust.
Israel.
No, lady.
This is not holocaust.
We should--
Yes.
We should oppose this term.
Now, abortion is a holocaust.
Armenians had a Holocaust.
In Bosnia, it is a Holocaust.
No.
The Holocaust is unique.
It is incomparable.
Holocaust is Jewish.
Now, call by whatever name you want to call it.
In Poland, 3 million ethnic Poles perished.
To my mind, I am Polish.
My people passed through crucifixion.
[INAUDIBLE], you know, martyrdom.
But it was not a holocaust.
Holocaust is Jewish.
There is basic difference.
I will give you this example.
If a German official saw on the streets of Warsaw, or Athens, or Sarajevo, or Prague, a local child, he would not hurt that child.
He would pass the child indifferently.
The child was to grow into a slave of the master race.
Now, if he saw a Jewish child, he would dispatch the child for destruction as a vermin of humanity.
This is Holocaust.
It's a different proposition.
You, you see that difference.
We should not use this word 'holocaust' to any other instances. 'Holocaust' means 'destruction of the Jews during the Second World War' period.
Call-- call whatever you want to call them.
Call them whatever you want.
And--
--both-- and Jews and non-Jews, certainly I, should oppose this.
We shouldn't allow that eventually this term 'holocaust' will mean-- one of those things, you know?
They happen.
They happen.
Or, Genghis Khan, also, was holocaust, you know.
Attila, also, holocaust, et cetera.
Holocaust should be remembered as it was, incomparable, unique, Jewish.
I don't know.
I don't know.
What do I know?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
--everything, in a way, is possible.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I am not as fanatically Catholic as I was when I was 15.
But I-- I am believer.
And I believe in this proposition mainly.
God gave us a soul.
Call it consciousness, whatever it is.
And we have infinite capacity to do good.
And every one of us has infinite capacity to do evil.
And we have a choice one or another.
And Almighty God is out of it.
He left us free with it, free decision.
Everything is possible as a result.
Everything is possible.
I had a brother who, when the war broke out-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] all of this, by the way, you will find in the book published by Wood.
My brother was very high in the government.
When the war broke out, he was a commandant of all security forces in Warsaw, police, [? open, ?] secret, et cetera.
He was arrested by Gestapo, because he created some conspiracy in the police.
The Germans discovered it.
And he was already arrested, May 1940, and sent to Auschwitz.
Because at that time, Auschwitz already existed.
Spent in Auschwitz 11 months, and then was released in 1941.
Joined underground immediately and worked through the end of the war in the Polish underground.
When the war ended, of course, he was afraid of his life.
Because of the communist regime-- went abroad, eventually came to the United States.
No.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
My father, I hardly remember him.
Only I remember my mother, who, as far as I remember, died 1934 or '35.
OK, we'll stop here.
[INAUDIBLE]
